mockallofus 0.0.0.9000
  • Get started
    • Reproducible and portable workflows
    • Developing a real pipeline locally: the HIPPS pregnancy algorithm
    • What's different from the Researcher Workbench
  • Reference

mockallofus

A local mock database for developing All of Us analyses without the paid Researcher Workbench.

The allofus R package wraps access to the All of Us Research Program data, which lives in Google BigQuery and is only reachable from the (metered) Researcher Workbench. That makes iterative code development — especially for students who rerun constantly — slow and expensive.

mockallofus builds a local DuckDB database whose tables and columns mirror the All of Us Curated Data Repository (OMOP CDM core tables plus the All of Us extension and cohort-builder tables), populated with fully synthetic, randomly generated data. You can then develop and test the same allofus analysis code locally that you would run on the Workbench.

The synthetic data is random and does not reflect real All of Us data (which would violate the data use agreement). Results are not meant to be realistic — the point is to make code run.

Installation

mockallofus currently requires the development version of allofus from the mock-duckdb-support branch, which adds the backend-aware query execution that lets allofus functions run against DuckDB rather than only BigQuery. (Once that work is merged and released, the released allofus will work.)

# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("roux-ohdsi/allofus@mock-duckdb-support")  # required allofus branch
pak::pak("louisahsmith/mockallofus")                # installs duckdb too

mockallofus declares this requirement in its Remotes: field, so installing it (or its dependencies, e.g. in CI) pulls the correct allofus branch automatically.

Usage

The only thing that changes versus a Workbench script is the connection call: use mock_aou_connect() instead of allofus::aou_connect(). Everything downstream is identical.

library(allofus)
library(mockallofus)
library(dplyr)

con <- mock_aou_connect()   # builds the database on first run, then caches it

# ...the same code you'd run on the Workbench:
tbl(con, "person") |> count() |> collect()

cohort <- tbl(con, "person") |> select(person_id)

aou_concept_set(cohort, concepts = c(201826, 4193704), domains = "condition",
                output = "indicator", concept_set_name = "t2dm")

aou_survey(questions = c(1585838, 1586135),
           question_output = c("gender", "birthplace"))

aou_observation_period()

Making your study’s queries return data

A small default database means queries for specific concepts or eligibility criteria can come back empty, which is harder to develop against than no data at all. Rather than making the database enormous, seed the concepts your study uses, at a prevalence you choose, so eligibility joins return partial cohorts:

# guarantee these condition concepts exist for ~30% of participants
mock_seed_concept_set(con, concepts = c(201826, 4193704),
                      domain = "condition", prevalence = 0.3)

# guarantee responses to specific survey questions (real codebook concept ids)
mock_seed_survey(con, concept_ids = c(1585838, 1586135), prevalence = 0.6)

Seeding is deterministic (for a fixed seed) and attaches to existing participants, so all downstream joins stay consistent.

What works locally, and what doesn’t

Because most allofus functions are pure dbplyr (which translates to DuckDB automatically) and the package’s internal query helper is backend-aware, the large majority of workflows run unchanged.

Works locally

  • dplyr/dbplyr on tbl(con, ...), aou_join(), aou_collect()
  • aou_concept_set()
  • aou_survey() (with real codebook question concept ids)
  • aou_observation_period()
  • aou_compute(), aou_create_temp_table()
  • aou_sql() — provided the SQL is portable. BigQuery-only constructs (COUNTIF, APPROX_QUANTILES, etc.) will not run; a few common ones (CONTAINS_SUBSTR, REGEXP_CONTAINS) are emulated via DuckDB macros.

Workbench-only (clearly-labeled exceptions)

  • aou_atlas_cohort() — relies on BigQuery-dialect SQL generated by SqlRender.
  • File/bucket management (aou_ls_workspace(), aou_ls_bucket(), aou_workspace_to_bucket(), aou_bucket_to_workspace()) — these use Google Cloud Storage, which has no local equivalent.
  • aou_session_info() CDR-metadata details.
  • User-written SQL that uses BigQuery-specific functions.

How it works

  • build_mock_db() creates every table in the All of Us data dictionary (from allofus::aou_table_info) and populates a core subset with synthetic rows. EHR-sourced rows are tagged in the _ext tables so EHR-based functions (like aou_observation_period()) behave correctly.
  • mock_aou_connect() opens the DuckDB database, registers a few BigQuery-compatibility macros, attaches BigQuery-compatible dbplyr translations, and stores the connection in getOption("aou.default.con") and a schema name in getOption("aou.default.cdr") — exactly the options allofus reads.
  • BigQuery-isms common in All of Us code are handled so pipelines run unchanged: the three-argument date_diff(a, b, sql("day")), as.Date() of float-valued concatenated date parts (e.g. "1994-5.0-28.0"), and the collect(page_size = ...) download argument. More complex real-world pipelines (e.g. the HIPPS pregnancy algorithm) run end-to-end on the mock database after seeding their concept lists.

Disclaimer

This project is not affiliated with or endorsed by the All of Us Research Program. The data it generates is synthetic and does not represent real participants.
